article_title: Nine CrackArmor Vulnerabilities in Linux AppArmor Expose 12.6 Million Servers to Root Takeover

content_preview.title: Nine CrackArmor Vulnerabilities in Linux AppArmor Expose 12.6 Million Servers to Root Takeover
content_preview.summary: Qualys discovers nine confused deputy flaws in AppArmor that have lurked since 2017, enabling unprivileged users to escalate to root, escape containers, and crash kernels across Ubuntu, Debian, and SUSE.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The Qualys Threat Research Unit has disclosed nine vulnerabilities in AppArmor, the mandatory access control module that ships enabled by default on Ubuntu, Debian, and SUSE Linux distributions. Collectively dubbed [CrackArmor](https://blog.qualys.com/vulnerabilities-threat-research/2026/03/12/crackarmor-critical-apparmor-flaws-enable-local-privilege-escalation-to-root), the flaws have existed in every Linux kernel since version 4.11, released in 2017, and affect an estimated 12.6 m...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Excellent cybersecurity news article at 685 words with strong technical depth. Well-organized with Overview, What We Know, What We Don't Know, and Analysis sections. The analysis section adds genuine insight about kernel security module auditing patterns.
editor_notes.source_verification: The Hacker News — ✅ Confirmed: nine CrackArmor flaws, confused deputy classification, Qualys TRU, Saeed Abbasi, 12.6M instances, kernel 4.11 since 2017, no CVEs assigned, PoC withheld, all attack vectors (priv esc, container escape, KASLR bypass, DoS, namespace bypass). Qualys blog — ✅ Confirmed: March 12 disclosure, nine vulns, confused deputy, /sys/kernel/security/apparmor/ pseudo-files, write vs open permission check, aa_loaddata use-after-free, 16KB stack exhaustion on x86-64, KASLR OOB reads, container escape via userns profiles. Ubuntu blog — ✅ Confirmed: Ubuntu 14.04 through 25.10 affected, Launchpad Bug #2143853, kernel patches + reboot, apt update/upgrade, sudo vulnerability chaining. Nuance: Ubuntu blog notes privilege escalation + container escape combo is absent on 14.04/16.04 specifically. blog.qualys.com and ubuntu.com not on allowlist — both are authoritative primary sources (vendor disclosure and OS vendor advisory).
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All claims verified against all three sources. Minor nuance: article says 'every supported Ubuntu release is affected from Trusty Tahr (14.04 LTS) through Questing Quokka (25.10)' — Ubuntu blog confirms all releases affected by some flaws, but notes the full priv-esc+container-escape chain is absent on 14.04/16.04. The article's phrasing is technically correct but could be more precise. Not a factual error.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. Technical depth is appropriate without being sensationalist. The Analysis section provides valuable context about kernel security auditing patterns without editorializing.
editor_notes.originality: No existing AppArmor or CrackArmor articles in the archive. Topic is entirely new.
recommendations: Add blog.qualys.com and ubuntu.com to source_allowlist.txt — both are authoritative primary sources for security disclosures and OS advisories
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Outstanding cybersecurity submission with thorough technical coverage verified against all three primary sources. The article excels at balancing accessibility with technical accuracy. Approved for publication.
